svg.aria-label: Animated diagram of a proposed segmented value log: sealed segments are immutable and their ids are never reused, so GC rewrites only the worst segment's survivors into a new segment, re-points the keys, flushes, and unlinks the old file. A reader holding an old pointer reads the same record or gets ENOENT and re-resolves — so the generation seqlock, the record-sequence check and the GC journal are all unnecessary.
svg.text: PROPOSED — segmented value log: immutable segments, ids never reused
GC's unit becomes one segment, not the whole bucket. An old pointer is stale, never wrong — which is what deletes the read-path race machinery.
POINTER (u128) — THE OLD 64-BIT page_offset FIELD, SPLIT
bucket (32b) │ segment_file_id (32b) — monotone, never reused │ page_off (32b) │ slot (32b)
A READER HOLDING ptr(seg 12, slot 1) WHILE ALL THIS HAPPENS

g.text: reads seg 12 → k1's own bytes, same seq → CORRECT (no seqlock, no seq check needed)
open(seg 12) → ENOENT → re-resolve from LSM → seg 15 → value (loud, local, retryable)

svg.text: idle — the pointer it resolved is still valid, whatever GC is doing
GC — NO JOURNAL, NO COMMIT MARKER, NO .new/.old SWAP

g.text: next_id = AtomicU64 (monotone, in-memory) ✓ next_id = max(existing files) + 1 ✗ — recycles 12
Sealed segments are immutable; only the tail (seg 14) is appended to. Segment ids are monotone and NEVER reused.
put(k2, v′) appends to the tail. The old record is NOT edited in place — the writer just adds its bytes to seg 12's garbage counter.
Another overwrite. Seg 12 is now 50% garbage; seg 13 is untouched and stays that way — GC never has to look at it.
GC picks the worst segment by garbage ratio — seg 12. No lock is taken yet, and no other segment is read or written.
Each record carries its KEY, so GC reads seg 12 sequentially and asks the LSM per record: k1 ✓ k4 ✓ survive; k2, k3 no longer point here → dropped.
Survivors are appended to a NEW segment (15) and fsynced. Both copies exist for now — that is the only transient duplication.
A reader still holding ptr(seg 12, slot 1) reads it anyway — and gets k1's OWN bytes, same sequence. An old pointer is stale, never wrong.
Now the bucket lock is taken — for a CAS re-point loop only, milliseconds, not a multi-GB copy. Seg 12 is now entirely unreferenced.
Flush the memtable to L0: the re-point is now DURABLE. This is the same rule WAL GC uses before deleting a segment.
Only now is seg 12 unlinked. Space returns to the filesystem as a whole file — no sparse holes, no st_blocks accounting.
A reader that resolved ptr(seg 12) before the unlink now gets ENOENT on open → it re-resolves from the LSM and reads seg 15. Loud, local, retryable.
Crash anywhere in that sequence and the durable LSM still points at a segment that exists → no GC journal, no commit marker, no .new/.old recovery.
THE LANDMINE: ids must come from a monotone in-memory counter. Deriving the next id from max(files)+1 would recycle 12 — and bring the whole race back.
Net: GC wrote 2 records to reclaim 4, touched one segment, and reads need no generation seqlock and no record-seq check — only an ENOENT retry.

svg.text: RECORDS ON DISK IN THIS BUCKET (LIVE vs GARBAGE)
Seg 13 is never read or written by GC — that is the write-amplification win.
